claim: Fermanal Fe-Mn-Al austenitic alloys have a density 12-15% below that of iron, can be precipitation hardened, and possess high mechanical properties and good oxidation resistance.
evidence_text: Austenitic alloys of the 'Fermanal' type (Fe—Mn—Al) have recently come into use abroad in the aviation and automotive industries for critical and heavily loaded parts (springs, gears, landing gears). These alloys have a relatively low density (12-15% below that of iron), are capable of precipitation hardening, and have high mechanical properties and good oxidation resistance.

claim: After quenching, alloy G30Yu9 exhibits ultimate tensile strength 84 kg/mm², yield strength 57 kg/mm², elongation 72%, and reduction of area 70%, as reported in Table 1.
evidence_text: The mechanical properties of the forged and heat treated alloy G30Yu9 [2] and the alloy with 26.1% Mn, 10.2% Al, and 1.02% C are given in Table 1. | Quenched . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .|84|57|72|70|

claim: Over 100 heats were prepared with compositions ranging from 5-45% Mn, 2-14% Al, and 0.05-1.5% C; melting in a laboratory induction furnace in helium after evacuation to 10^-3 mm Hg; investment cast samples 65 mm long, 11 mm diameter.
evidence_text: Over 100 heats were prepared with 5-45% Mn, 2-14% Al, and 0.05-1.5% C. The alloys were melted in a laboratory induction furnace in a helium atmosphere (previously evacuated to 10^{-3} mm Hg) and in a 30-kg commercial furnace. Samples 65 mm long and 11 mm in diam. were produced by investment casting.

claim: Figure 1 presents the iron corner of the Fe-Mn-Al diagram with ~1% C at 1150°C; alloys below line ab exhibit single-phase γ, while those above have two-phase γ+α structure (see also Fig.2 a, I and II).
evidence_text: Figure 1 shows the iron corner of the phase diagram for Fe—Mn—Al alloys with about 1% at 1150°C drawn on the basis of the experiments. After quenching from 1150°C the alloys below line ab have a single-phase structure (Fig.2, a, I) and above the line a two-phase structure consisting of α- and γ-solid solutions. (Fig.2, a, II).

claim: In the alloy with 30% Mn and 10% Al after quenching, a single-phase α-solid solution forms at ~0.05% C, a two-phase structure at 0.25% C, and a single-phase γ-solid solution at 1% C, with the α-phase fraction decreasing as carbon increases.
evidence_text: The alloy with 30% Mn and 10% Al has (after quenching) a single-phase structure of α-solid solution at ~ 0.05% C and a two-phase structure with 0.25% C. The amount of α-phase decreases with increasing carbon, and at 1% C the structure is single-phase, consisting of γ-solid solution.

claim: For alloys with ~9% Al, the ultimate tensile strength and yield strength increase with aging temperature up to 550°C, while plasticity and ductility decrease sharply; above 550°C strength decreases and plasticity increases due to coalescence of the dispersed phase, as shown in Fig.3.
evidence_text: In alloys with ~9% Al the ultimate strength and yield strength increase with the aging temperature up to 550°C, but the plasticity and ductility decline sharply (Fig.3). Above 550°C the strength begins to decrease and the plasticity to increase due to coalescence of the dispersed phase.

claim: As an example from Table 2, the alloy with 0.93% C, 23.7% Mn, 8.2% Al has magnetic permeability 1.008 G/Oe in the quenched state and 1.900 G/Oe after quenching and aging 20 h at 600°C.
Alloy density decreases from 7.24 to 6.68 g/cm³ when aluminum increases from 6 to 10%; alloys with 9% Al and 25-30% Mn have density 6.75-6.85 g/cm³.
evidence_text: With increasing aluminum concentrations from 6 to 10% the density decreased from 7.24 to 6.68 g/cm^3. Alloys with 9% Al and 25-30% Mn had a density of 6.75-6.85 g/cm^3.
